insights: Database category has the longest median resolution time at 3921.77 hours (163.4 days), 30.86% above the overall median, and contributes 22.72% of total resolution hours despite representing only 19.92% of records—indicating it is a structural driver of prolonged resolution cycles rather than a statistical outlier.

Database's elevated resolution times are inherent to the category itself, not attributable to specific assignees: Fred Luddy’s Database tickets take 87.4% longer than his non-Database work, and Beth Anglin’s Database tickets are 45.4% slower than her other-category work—confirming category-level complexity as the primary cause.

Critical-priority tickets have the longest median resolution time (3358.69 hours, +12.07% above overall median), while Low-priority tickets resolve fastest (2876.69 hours, −4.01%), and this counterintuitive pattern holds across all categories—including Database (smallest gap, +7.84%) and Network (largest gap, +164.53%)—suggesting priority assignment does not align with operational capacity or triage efficiency.

Resolution times improved dramatically over 2023: median dropped from 4855 hours in Q1 to 809 hours in Q4 (−83.34%), with Winter (4952.10h) worst and Fall (1077.25h) best—revealing that temporal factors dominate magnitude shifts, and current performance cannot be interpreted without accounting for this steep downward trend.

Monthly ticket volume strongly correlates with resolution time (r = 0.880): high-volume months (e.g., January: 39 tickets, median 4555h) show severely delayed closures, while low-volume months (e.g., October–December: 3–9 tickets, median ~1000–1500h) enable rapid resolution—indicating systemic capacity saturation during demand peaks.

The longest-resolution combination is Network × Charlie Whitherspoon (4993.79h median, 11 tickets), not a Database pairing—highlighting that cross-category interaction effects can override category-level expectations and expose hidden bottlenecks in specific agent-domain alignments.
summary: The analysis confirms that Database category is the dominant structural factor driving prolonged resolution times—its 30.86% above-median duration reflects intrinsic complexity, not assignee performance, as evidenced by consistent slowdowns across multiple agents. Critically, priority level shows a counterintuitive inverse relationship with speed, suggesting misalignment between urgency labeling and actual resolution capacity. Temporal trends reveal an overwhelming contextual effect: resolution times collapsed by 83% from Q1 to Q4 2023, with seasonal variation spanning 161 days between worst (Winter) and best (Fall) periods—meaning any static category-level comparison must be anchored to this steep improvement curve. Strong correlation between monthly volume and resolution time (r = 0.880) further indicates that demand surges—not just category or priority—trigger acute capacity strain. Next, focus should shift to diagnosing why Critical tickets remain slowest despite urgency, validating whether Database’s complexity maps to specific sub-tasks or dependencies, and assessing whether recent gains are sustainable across high-volume windows.
